name: Zerodha Live Trading Dashboard — GraphQL Subscriptions
scenario: Zerodha's Kite app refreshes prices by polling every 500ms — that's 10,000 HTTP requests/second for 5000 users. Build GraphQL subscriptions to push price updates and trade confirmations over WebSocket, reducing server load by 80%.

core.description: PriceUpdate record (symbol, price, change, timestamp) and Trade record (id, symbol, qty, price, type, accountId) with isFinal() if type is TRADE_COMPLETE.
@SubscriptionMapping priceUpdate(@Argument String symbol): Flux.interval(1s) emitting PriceUpdate with symbol, simulated price drift. No takeUntil (continuous feed).
@SubscriptionMapping tradeUpdates(@Argument String accountId): filter TradeEventBus by accountId. doOnSubscribe/doOnCancel for registry.
SubscriberRegistry @Service: AtomicInteger per subscription key. register(key)/unregister(key)/count(key) methods.
@SpringBootTest (4 tests): priceUpdate emits symbol correctly, tradeUpdates filtered by accountId, other account's trades ignored, registry count tracked. All pass.

bonus.description: Auth validation: @Argument String userId validated before returning Flux. Invalid userId → Flux.error(SecurityException). Valid → normal stream. Test: invalid userId errors, valid works.
PaymentStatus subscription: filter by paymentId + .takeUntil(status::isFinal). Test: PLACED+CAPTURED=2 events, EXTRA after CAPTURED not received, stream completes.
Backpressure demo: priceStream with .onBackpressureDrop(), paymentStream with .onBackpressureBuffer(100). Document: when to use each. Test: both compile and run.

ai_policy_explanation: 5 hints. @SubscriptionMapping returns Flux<T>: Spring for GraphQL automatically maps each Flux emission to a WebSocket message. Sinks.many().multicast(): allows multiple subscribers (multiple users watching same bus) — each gets all events. .filter(t -> t.accountId().equals(accountId)): every subscriber has their own filter (server-side routing). .takeUntil(predicate): inclusive — the matching element IS emitted, then Flux completes. StepVerifier.create(flux).then(() -> publish()).expectNext(): .then() runs AFTER subscription setup but BEFORE expecting events — critical for Sinks-based tests.
